name: planner
content:
**Plan:**

- Step 1:
      * sub-task: Data Preparation, Noise Mitigation, and Core Statistical Computations
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load both datasets (`point_vortex_tracers.npy` and `levy_walk_trajectories.npy`)
        - Apply Savitzky-Golay filter to `x_noisy`/`y_noisy` fields for each tracer/trajectory separately; validate against `x_true`/`y_true` by computing RMSE before and after filtering
        - Compute Time-Averaged MSD (TAMSD) for each individual tracer (point-vortex) and trajectory (Lévy walk) across all lag times using the true positions
        - Fit power-law slopes (anomalous exponent α) to each TAMSD curve using log-log linear regression over the intermediate time range (avoiding very short and very long lags)
        - Compute ensemble-averaged MSD and TAMSD per configuration (N ∈ {5,10,20,40} for point-vortex; β ∈ {1.2,1.5,1.8,2.5} for Lévy walk)
        - Compute single-step displacement increments Δx, Δy for both datasets per configuration
        - Fit Lévy-stable distributions to the displacement increment tails (using Hill estimator or scipy stable fit) to extract tail exponent μ per configuration
        - Compute the ergodicity-breaking parameter EB(Δt) = Var(TAMSD)/Mean(TAMSD)² as a function of lag time for all configurations
        - Save all computed results (TAMSD arrays, α values, μ values, EB arrays, filtered positions) to disk as numpy/npz files for use in subsequent steps

- Step 2:
      * sub-task: Characteristic Function Fitting and Fractional Diffusion Modeling
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load the displacement increments and trajectory data saved in Step 1
        - For each configuration (N values and β values), compute the empirical characteristic function φ(k, Δt) = ⟨exp(ik·Δx)⟩ averaged over all tracers/trajectories, for a range of lag times Δt and wavenumbers k
        - Fit the fractional diffusion model φ(k, Δt) = exp(-D_α |k|^α Δt) to the empirical characteristic functions at each lag time Δt, extracting α(Δt) and D_α(Δt)
        - Identify the crossover time τ_c for each point-vortex configuration as the lag time where α(Δt) stabilizes (derivative falls below threshold) or enters the 95% CI of the corresponding Lévy walk α
        - Use bootstrapping across tracer ensembles to compute uncertainty bounds on τ_c and on the fitted α values
        - Save all characteristic function fits, α(Δt) profiles, D_α(Δt) profiles, crossover times, and bootstrap confidence intervals to disk

- Step 3:
      * sub-task: Effective Theory Mapping and KS Statistical Validation
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load all computed results from Steps 1 and 2
        - Construct the effective theory mapping: for each point-vortex N configuration, identify the Lévy walk β class whose asymptotic α and tail exponent μ most closely match; quantify the match using Euclidean distance in (α, μ) space
        - Perform Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests comparing point-vortex displacement distributions at crossover time τ_c against the matched Lévy walk displacement distributions
        - Compute the theoretical prediction for α from vortex density using the corrected formula (accounting for finite N and circulation variance γ_std=1.0), and compare against empirical α(N)
        - Tabulate all key results: α_emp(N), μ(N), τ_c(N), matched β, KS statistic and p-value, EB plateau values, D_α(N)
        - Save all tabulated results and mapping data to disk

- Step 4:
      * sub-task: Generate All Figures
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load all saved results from Steps 1–3
        - **Figure 1:** Multi-panel plot showing ensemble-averaged TAMSD vs lag time for all point-vortex N configurations and all Lévy walk β classes on log-log axes, with fitted power-law slopes annotated
        - **Figure 2:** Multi-panel plot of displacement increment PDFs (Δx) for point-vortex (all N) and Lévy walk (all β) on log-log axes, with fitted Lévy-stable tails overlaid and tail exponent μ annotated
        - **Figure 3:** α(Δt) profiles from characteristic function fitting for all point-vortex N configurations, showing crossover time τ_c marked, with bootstrap confidence bands; include horizontal reference lines for Lévy walk theoretical α values
        - **Figure 4:** Effective theory mapping plot — scatter plot of empirical (α_emp, μ) for point-vortex configurations vs Lévy walk classes, with connecting lines showing the best-match mapping; include theoretical prediction curve
        - **Figure 5:** Ergodicity-breaking parameter EB(Δt) vs lag time for all configurations (point-vortex and Lévy walk), on log-log axes
        - Save all figures at high resolution (dpi≥300)

- Step 5:
      * sub-task: Write Full Results Section
      * agent: researcher
      * bullet points:
        - Synthesize all quantitative results from Steps 1–4 into a comprehensive Results section of a scientific paper
        - Report noise mitigation validation results (RMSE before/after Savitzky-Golay filtering)
        - Report TAMSD power-law fits: α_emp for each N (point-vortex) and each β (Lévy walk), with uncertainties; discuss whether α increases monotonically with N
        - Report displacement increment PDF tail exponents μ for all configurations; compare point-vortex tails to Lévy walk tails
        - Report characteristic function fitting results: α(Δt) profiles, crossover times τ_c(N) with bootstrap uncertainties, D_α(N) values
        - Report the effective theory mapping: which Lévy walk β class best matches each point-vortex N configuration, with KS test statistics and p-values
        - Report theoretical prediction of α from vortex density and compare to empirical values
        - Report ergodicity-breaking parameter EB profiles and discuss implications for ergodicity in point-vortex systems vs Lévy walks
        - Reference all figures by number and describe key visual features
        - Provide physical interpretation of all results in the context of the Kirchhoff-Onsager Hamiltonian and anomalous transport theory

name: planner_response_evaluator
content:
**PLAN**

- Step 1:
	* sub-task: Data Preparation, Noise Mitigation, and Core Statistical Computations
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load both datasets (point_vortex_tracers.npy and levy_walk_trajectories.npy)
		- Apply Savitzky-Golay filter to x_noisy/y_noisy fields for each tracer/trajectory separately; validate against x_true/y_true by computing RMSE before and after filtering
		- Compute Time-Averaged MSD (TAMSD) for each individual tracer (point-vortex) and trajectory (Lévy walk) across all lag times using the true positions
		- Fit power-law slopes (anomalous exponent α) to each TAMSD curve using log-log linear regression over the intermediate time range (avoiding very short and very long lags)
		- Compute ensemble-averaged MSD and TAMSD per configuration (N ∈ {5,10,20,40} for point-vortex; β ∈ {1.2,1.5,1.8,2.5} for Lévy walk)
		- Compute single-step displacement increments Δx, Δy for both datasets per configuration
		- Fit Lévy-stable distributions to the displacement increment tails (using Hill estimator or scipy stable fit) to extract tail exponent μ per configuration
		- Compute the ergodicity-breaking parameter EB(Δt) = Var(TAMSD)/Mean(TAMSD)² as a function of lag time for all configurations
		- Save all computed results (TAMSD arrays, α values, μ values, EB arrays, filtered positions) to disk as numpy/npz files for use in subsequent steps

- Step 2:
	* sub-task: Characteristic Function Fitting and Fractional Diffusion Modeling
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load the displacement increments and trajectory data saved in Step 1
		- For each configuration (N values and β values), compute the empirical characteristic function φ(k, Δt) = ⟨exp(ik·Δx)⟩ averaged over all tracers/trajectories, for a range of lag times Δt and wavenumbers k
		- Fit the fractional diffusion model φ(k, Δt) = exp(-D_α |k|^α Δt) to the empirical characteristic functions at each lag time Δt, extracting α(Δt) and D_α(Δt)
		- Identify the crossover time τ_c for each point-vortex configuration as the lag time where α(Δt) stabilizes (derivative falls below threshold) or enters the 95% CI of the corresponding Lévy walk α
		- Use bootstrapping across tracer ensembles to compute uncertainty bounds on τ_c and on the fitted α values
		- Save all characteristic function fits, α(Δt) profiles, D_α(Δt) profiles, crossover times, and bootstrap confidence intervals to disk

- Step 3:
	* sub-task: Effective Theory Mapping and KS Statistical Validation
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load all computed results from Steps 1 and 2
		- Construct the effective theory mapping: for each point-vortex N configuration, identify the Lévy walk β class whose asymptotic α and tail exponent μ most closely match; quantify the match using Euclidean distance in (α, μ) space
		- Perform Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests comparing point-vortex displacement distributions at crossover time τ_c against the matched Lévy walk displacement distributions
		- Compute the theoretical prediction for α from vortex density using the corrected formula (accounting for finite N and circulation variance γ_std=1.0), and compare against empirical α(N)
		- Tabulate all key results: α_emp(N), μ(N), τ_c(N), matched β, KS statistic and p-value, EB plateau values, D_α(N)
		- Save all tabulated results and mapping data to disk

- Step 4:
	* sub-task: Generate All Figures
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load all saved results from Steps 1–3
		- Figure 1: Multi-panel plot showing ensemble-averaged TAMSD vs lag time for all point-vortex N configurations and all Lévy walk β classes on log-log axes, with fitted power-law slopes annotated
		- Figure 2: Multi-panel plot of displacement increment PDFs (Δx) for point-vortex (all N) and Lévy walk (all β) on log-log axes, with fitted Lévy-stable tails overlaid and tail exponent μ annotated
		- Figure 3: α(Δt) profiles from characteristic function fitting for all point-vortex N configurations, showing crossover time τ_c marked, with bootstrap confidence bands; include horizontal reference lines for Lévy walk theoretical α values
		- Figure 4: Effective theory mapping plot — scatter plot of empirical (α_emp, μ) for point-vortex configurations vs Lévy walk classes, with connecting lines showing the best-match mapping; include theoretical prediction curve
		- Figure 5: Ergodicity-breaking parameter EB(Δt) vs lag time for all configurations (point-vortex and Lévy walk), on log-log axes
		- Save all figures at high resolution (dpi≥300)

- Step 5:
	* sub-task: Write Full Results Section
	* agent in charge: researcher

	* instructions:
		- Synthesize all quantitative results from Steps 1–4 into a comprehensive Results section of a scientific paper
		- Report noise mitigation validation results (RMSE before/after Savitzky-Golay filtering)
		- Report TAMSD power-law fits: α_emp for each N (point-vortex) and each β (Lévy walk), with uncertainties; discuss whether α increases monotonically with N
		- Report displacement increment PDF tail exponents μ for all configurations; compare point-vortex tails to Lévy walk tails
		- Report characteristic function fitting results: α(Δt) profiles, crossover times τ_c(N) with bootstrap uncertainties, D_α(N) values
		- Report the effective theory mapping: which Lévy walk β class best matches each point-vortex N configuration, with KS test statistics and p-values
		- Report theoretical prediction of α from vortex density and compare to empirical values
		- Report ergodicity-breaking parameter EB profiles and discuss implications for ergodicity in point-vortex systems vs Lévy walks
		- Reference all figures by number and describe key visual features
		- Provide physical interpretation of all results in the context of the Kirchhoff-Onsager Hamiltonian and anomalous transport theory

name: plan_reviewer
content:
**Recommendations:**

- In Step 1, the instruction to fit Lévy-stable distributions using "Hill estimator or scipy stable fit" is ambiguous and may lead to inconsistent results across configurations. Specify that the Hill estimator should be applied to the absolute displacement increments using the top 10–15% of the empirical tail, and that the resulting tail exponent μ should be reported alongside a goodness-of-fit metric (e.g., mean absolute error of the log-log tail fit) to allow downstream comparison in Step 3.

- In Step 1, the TAMSD computation instruction does not specify the range of lag times to use for the power-law fit. Add an explicit instruction to restrict the log-log regression to lag times between 10% and 60% of the total trajectory duration, which avoids the noise-dominated short-lag regime and the poor-statistics long-lag regime, and to report the R² of each fit as a quality indicator.

- In Step 2, the characteristic function fitting instruction does not specify the range of wavenumbers k to use. Add an instruction to restrict the fit to the low-k regime (e.g., |k| ≤ k_max where the empirical characteristic function remains above a noise floor threshold such as |φ| > 0.1), since the fractional diffusion model is only valid in the small-k limit and fitting at large k will produce biased α estimates.

- In Step 2, the bootstrapping procedure for uncertainty bounds on τ_c is underspecified. Add an instruction to perform at least 200 bootstrap resamples of the tracer ensemble (sampling with replacement), recomputing the full α(Δt) profile for each resample, and to report the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles as the 95% confidence interval on both α(Δt) and τ_c.

- In Step 3, the theoretical prediction for α from vortex density is mentioned but the formula is not specified. Add an instruction to derive the corrected prediction using the relation α = 3 − μ_eff where μ_eff = 2 − δ(N, γ_std) is the effective tail index corrected for finite-N effects, with δ estimated empirically from the Hill estimator results of Step 1, and to plot the theoretical curve α_theory(N) alongside the empirical α_emp(N) in Figure 4.

- In Step 4, Figure 3 should also display the D_α(Δt) profiles (generalized diffusion coefficient as a function of lag time) for all point-vortex N configurations on a separate panel within the same figure, since the stabilization of D_α(Δt) is an independent indicator of the crossover to asymptotic fractional diffusion and provides complementary information to the α(Δt) profiles.

- In Step 4, Figure 2 should use both linear-scale inset panels (showing the central Gaussian-like core) and log-log axes (showing the power-law tails) for the displacement increment PDFs, so that the transition from Gaussian core to heavy-tailed behavior is visually explicit and the fitted tail exponents can be clearly distinguished from the bulk of the distribution.

- In Step 5, the researcher should explicitly compare the empirically derived crossover times τ_c(N) against the vortex interaction timescale estimated from the Kirchhoff-Onsager Hamiltonian (e.g., τ_KO ~ L²/Γ_rms where L is the domain size and Γ_rms is the RMS circulation), providing a physical interpretation of why τ_c decreases or increases with N and connecting the crossover to the onset of chaotic mixing in the vortex system.

- In Step 5, the researcher should include a dedicated subsection on the limitations of the effective theory mapping, specifically addressing the finite ensemble size (only 5 tracers per point-vortex configuration), the finite simulation time relative to the crossover time, and the sensitivity of the KS test p-values to the choice of displacement lag time used for the comparison, so that the reader can assess the robustness of the Lévy-equivalence claims.

name: reviewer_response_evaluator
content:
**Recommendations:**

In Step 1, the instruction to fit Lévy-stable distributions using 'Hill estimator or scipy stable fit' is ambiguous and may lead to inconsistent results across configurations. Specify that the Hill estimator should be applied to the absolute displacement increments using the top 10–15% of the empirical tail, and that the resulting tail exponent μ should be reported alongside a goodness-of-fit metric (e.g., mean absolute error of the log-log tail fit) to allow downstream comparison in Step 3.

In Step 1, the TAMSD computation instruction does not specify the range of lag times to use for the power-law fit. Add an explicit instruction to restrict the log-log regression to lag times between 10% and 60% of the total trajectory duration, which avoids the noise-dominated short-lag regime and the poor-statistics long-lag regime, and to report the R² of each fit as a quality indicator.

In Step 2, the characteristic function fitting instruction does not specify the range of wavenumbers k to use. Add an instruction to restrict the fit to the low-k regime (e.g., |k| ≤ k_max where the empirical characteristic function remains above a noise floor threshold such as |φ| > 0.1), since the fractional diffusion model is only valid in the small-k limit and fitting at large k will produce biased α estimates.

In Step 3, the theoretical prediction for α from vortex density is mentioned but the formula is not specified. Add an instruction to derive the corrected prediction using the relation α = 3 − μ_eff where μ_eff = 2 − δ(N, γ_std) is the effective tail index corrected for finite-N effects, with δ estimated empirically from the Hill estimator results of Step 1, and to plot the theoretical curve α_theory(N) alongside the empirical α_emp(N) in Figure 4.

In Step 4, Figure 3 should also display the D_α(Δt) profiles (generalized diffusion coefficient as a function of lag time) for all point-vortex N configurations on a separate panel within the same figure, since the stabilization of D_α(Δt) is an independent indicator of the crossover to asymptotic fractional diffusion and provides complementary information to the α(Δt) profiles.

In Step 4, Figure 2 should use both linear-scale inset panels (showing the central Gaussian-like core) and log-log axes (showing the power-law tails) for the displacement increment PDFs, so that the transition from Gaussian core to heavy-tailed behavior is visually explicit and the fitted tail exponents can be clearly distinguished from the bulk of the distribution.

In Step 5, the researcher should explicitly compare the empirically derived crossover times τ_c(N) against the vortex interaction timescale estimated from the Kirchhoff-Onsager Hamiltonian (e.g., τ_KO ~ L²/Γ_rms where L is the domain size and Γ_rms is the RMS circulation), providing a physical interpretation of why τ_c decreases or increases with N and connecting the crossover to the onset of chaotic mixing in the vortex system.

In Step 5, the researcher should include a dedicated subsection on the limitations of the effective theory mapping, specifically addressing the finite ensemble size (only 5 tracers per point-vortex configuration), the finite simulation time relative to the crossover time, and the sensitivity of the KS test p-values to the choice of displacement lag time used for the comparison, so that the reader can assess the robustness of the Lévy-equivalence claims.

name: review_recorder
content:
Recommendations have been logged.
Number of feedback rounds left: 0.
Now, update the plan accordingly, planner!

name: planner
content:
**Plan:**

- Step 1:
      * sub-task: Data Preparation, Noise Mitigation, and Core Statistical Computations
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load both datasets (`point_vortex_tracers.npy` and `levy_walk_trajectories.npy`)
        - Apply Savitzky-Golay filter to `x_noisy`/`y_noisy` fields for each tracer/trajectory separately; validate against `x_true`/`y_true` by computing RMSE before and after filtering for each configuration
        - Compute Time-Averaged MSD (TAMSD) for each individual tracer (point-vortex) and trajectory (Lévy walk) across all lag times using the true positions
        - Fit power-law slopes (anomalous exponent α) to each TAMSD curve using log-log linear regression restricted to lag times between 10% and 60% of total trajectory duration; report R² for each fit as a quality indicator
        - Compute ensemble-averaged MSD and TAMSD per configuration (N ∈ {5,10,20,40} for point-vortex; β ∈ {1.2,1.5,1.8,2.5} for Lévy walk)
        - Compute single-step displacement increments Δx, Δy for both datasets per configuration
        - Apply the Hill estimator to the absolute displacement increments using the top 10–15% of the empirical tail to extract tail exponent μ per configuration; report the mean absolute error of the log-log tail fit as a goodness-of-fit metric
        - Compute the ergodicity-breaking parameter EB(Δt) = Var(TAMSD)/Mean(TAMSD)² as a function of lag time for all configurations
        - Save all computed results (TAMSD arrays, α values with R², μ values with MAE, EB arrays, filtered positions, displacement increments) to disk as numpy/npz files

- Step 2:
      * sub-task: Characteristic Function Fitting and Fractional Diffusion Modeling
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load the displacement increments and trajectory data saved in Step 1
        - For each configuration (N values and β values), compute the empirical characteristic function φ(k, Δt) = ⟨exp(ik·Δx)⟩ averaged over all tracers/trajectories, for a range of lag times Δt and wavenumbers k
        - Restrict the characteristic function fitting to the low-k regime where |φ(k, Δt)| > 0.1 (noise floor threshold), since the fractional diffusion model φ(k, Δt) = exp(-D_α |k|^α Δt) is only valid in the small-k limit
        - Extract α(Δt) and D_α(Δt) from the fits at each lag time for all configurations
        - Identify the crossover time τ_c for each point-vortex configuration as the lag time where α(Δt) stabilizes (derivative falls below 5% of initial value) or enters the 95% CI of the corresponding Lévy walk α
        - Perform at least 200 bootstrap resamples of the tracer ensemble (sampling with replacement), recomputing the full α(Δt) profile for each resample; report the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles as the 95% confidence interval on both α(Δt) and τ_c
        - Save all characteristic function fits, α(Δt) profiles, D_α(Δt) profiles, crossover times, and bootstrap confidence intervals to disk

- Step 3:
      * sub-task: Effective Theory Mapping and KS Statistical Validation
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load all computed results from Steps 1 and 2
        - Construct the effective theory mapping: for each point-vortex N configuration, identify the Lévy walk β class whose asymptotic α and tail exponent μ most closely match; quantify the match using Euclidean distance in (α, μ) space
        - Perform Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests comparing point-vortex displacement distributions at crossover time τ_c against the matched Lévy walk displacement distributions
        - Compute the theoretical prediction for α from vortex density using the corrected formula α_theory = 3 − μ_eff where μ_eff = 2 − δ(N, γ_std), with δ estimated empirically from the Hill estimator results of Step 1; compare against empirical α_emp(N)
        - Tabulate all key results: α_emp(N), μ(N), τ_c(N) with bootstrap CI, matched β, KS statistic and p-value, EB plateau values, D_α(N), α_theory(N)
        - Save all tabulated results and mapping data to disk

- Step 4:
      * sub-task: Generate All Figures
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load all saved results from Steps 1–3
        - **Figure 1:** Multi-panel plot showing ensemble-averaged TAMSD vs lag time for all point-vortex N configurations (left panel) and all Lévy walk β classes (right panel) on log-log axes, with fitted power-law slopes and R² values annotated
        - **Figure 2:** Multi-panel plot of displacement increment PDFs (Δx) for point-vortex (all N) and Lévy walk (all β) on log-log axes with fitted Lévy-stable tails and tail exponent μ annotated; include linear-scale inset panels showing the central Gaussian-like core for each configuration
        - **Figure 3:** Two-panel figure — top panel shows α(Δt) profiles from characteristic function fitting for all point-vortex N configurations with crossover time τ_c marked and bootstrap 95% confidence bands, plus horizontal reference lines for Lévy walk theoretical α values; bottom panel shows D_α(Δt) profiles for all point-vortex N configurations
        - **Figure 4:** Effective theory mapping scatter plot of empirical (α_emp, μ) for point-vortex configurations vs Lévy walk classes, with connecting lines showing the best-match mapping and the theoretical prediction curve α_theory(N) = 3 − μ_eff(N) overlaid
        - **Figure 5:** Ergodicity-breaking parameter EB(Δt) vs lag time for all configurations (point-vortex and Lévy walk) on log-log axes
        - Save all figures at high resolution (dpi≥300)

- Step 5:
      * sub-task: Write Full Results Section
      * agent: researcher
      * bullet points:
        - Synthesize all quantitative results from Steps 1–4 into a comprehensive Results section of a scientific paper
        - Report noise mitigation validation results (RMSE before/after Savitzky-Golay filtering for each configuration)
        - Report TAMSD power-law fits: α_emp for each N (point-vortex) and each β (Lévy walk) with R² values and uncertainties; discuss whether α increases monotonically with N
        - Report displacement increment PDF tail exponents μ with MAE goodness-of-fit metrics for all configurations; compare point-vortex tails to Lévy walk tails
        - Report characteristic function fitting results: α(Δt) profiles, crossover times τ_c(N) with bootstrap 95% CI, D_α(N) values and their stabilization behavior
        - Report the effective theory mapping: which Lévy walk β class best matches each point-vortex N configuration, with KS test statistics and p-values
        - Report theoretical prediction α_theory(N) = 3 − μ_eff(N) and compare to empirical values, discussing the role of finite-N corrections δ(N, γ_std)
        - Report ergodicity-breaking parameter EB profiles and discuss implications for ergodicity in point-vortex systems vs Lévy walks
        - Explicitly compare crossover times τ_c(N) against the vortex interaction timescale τ_KO ~ L²/Γ_rms from the Kirchhoff-Onsager Hamiltonian, providing physical interpretation of the crossover
        - Include a dedicated subsection on limitations: finite ensemble size (5 tracers per configuration), finite simulation time relative to τ_c, and sensitivity of KS p-values to lag time choice
        - Reference all figures by number and describe key visual features
        - Provide physical interpretation of all results in the context of the Kirchhoff-Onsager Hamiltonian and anomalous transport theory

		- Load all saved results from Steps 1–3
		- Figure 1: Multi-panel plot showing ensemble-averaged TAMSD vs lag time for all point-vortex N configurations (left panel) and all Lévy walk β classes (right panel) on log-log axes, with fitted power-law slopes and R² values annotated
		- Figure 2: Multi-panel plot of displacement increment PDFs (Δx) for point-vortex (all N) and Lévy walk (all β) on log-log axes with fitted Lévy-stable tails and tail exponent μ annotated; include linear-scale inset panels showing the central Gaussian-like core for each configuration
		- Figure 3: Two-panel figure — top panel shows α(Δt) profiles from characteristic function fitting for all point-vortex N configurations with crossover time τ_c marked and bootstrap 95% confidence bands, plus horizontal reference lines for Lévy walk theoretical α values; bottom panel shows D_α(Δt) profiles for all point-vortex N configurations
		- Figure 4: Effective theory mapping scatter plot of empirical (α_emp, μ) for point-vortex configurations vs Lévy walk classes, with connecting lines showing the best-match mapping and the theoretical prediction curve α_theory(N) = 3 − μ_eff(N) overlaid
		- Figure 5: Ergodicity-breaking parameter EB(Δt) vs lag time for all configurations (point-vortex and Lévy walk) on log-log axes
		- Save all figures at high resolution (dpi≥300)

- Step 5:
	* sub-task: Write Full Results Section
	* agent in charge: researcher

	* instructions:
		- Synthesize all quantitative results from Steps 1–4 into a comprehensive Results section of a scientific paper
		- Report noise mitigation validation results (RMSE before/after Savitzky-Golay filtering for each configuration)
		- Report TAMSD power-law fits: α_emp for each N (point-vortex) and each β (Lévy walk) with R² values and uncertainties; discuss whether α increases monotonically with N
		- Report displacement increment PDF tail exponents μ with MAE goodness-of-fit metrics for all configurations; compare point-vortex tails to Lévy walk tails
		- Report characteristic function fitting results: α(Δt) profiles, crossover times τ_c(N) with bootstrap 95% CI, D_α(N) values and their stabilization behavior
		- Report the effective theory mapping: which Lévy walk β class best matches each point-vortex N configuration, with KS test statistics and p-values
		- Report theoretical prediction α_theory(N) = 3 − μ_eff(N) and compare to empirical values, discussing the role of finite-N corrections δ(N, γ_std)
		- Report ergodicity-breaking parameter EB profiles and discuss implications for ergodicity in point-vortex systems vs Lévy walks
		- Explicitly compare crossover times τ_c(N) against the vortex interaction timescale τ_KO ~ L²/Γ_rms from the Kirchhoff-Onsager Hamiltonian, providing physical interpretation of the crossover
		- Include a dedicated subsection on limitations: finite ensemble size (5 tracers per configuration), finite simulation time relative to τ_c, and sensitivity of KS p-values to lag time choice
		- Reference all figures by number and describe key visual features
		- Provide physical interpretation of all results in the context of the Kirchhoff-Onsager Hamiltonian and anomalous transport theory
